skill.name: data-exploration
skill.description: Systematic database and table profiling for DBX Studio. Use when a user wants to understand their data, explore schema structure, or profile a dataset.
skill.summary: Systematic database and table profiling for DBX Studio. Use when a user wants to understand their data, explore schema structure, or profile a dataset.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 19 potential issues (external commands, weak crypto, system reconnaissance). All findings are false positives caused by pattern matching on markdown code blocks containing SQL queries. The skill contains only documentation and SQL query templates for database profiling - no executable code, no network access, no file system operations, no environment variable access. This is a legitimate data exploration guide.

content.user_title: Explore and Profile Your Database Data
content.value_statement: Understanding unfamiliar databases takes hours of manual querying. This skill automates schema discovery, data profiling, and quality assessment so you can quickly comprehend any dataset.
actual_capabilities: Automatically discover all tables in a database schema using read_schema
Profile individual tables for row counts, column types, and data distributions
Identify foreign key relationships and join patterns between tables
Calculate data quality metrics including null rates and completeness scores
Generate summary reports with tables, relationships, quality flags, and suggested queries
Provide SQL query templates for common profiling tasks
limitations: Requires existing database connection in DBX Studio - cannot connect to databases directly
Only analyzes schemas accessible through the connected database user permissions
Does not modify database structure or data - read-only exploration and profiling
Performance depends on database size - profiling very large tables may take time
Cannot detect application-level relationships not enforced as foreign keys

use_cases.title: Onboard to a New Database
use_cases.description: Quickly understand the structure and contents of an unfamiliar database by discovering tables, profiling columns, and identifying relationships.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineer starting a new role

use_cases.title: Assess Data Quality Before Analysis
use_cases.description: Evaluate completeness and null rates across columns to identify data quality issues before starting analytics or machine learning projects.
use_cases.target_user: Data scientist preparing dataset for analysis

use_cases.title: Document Legacy Database Schema
use_cases.description: Generate comprehensive documentation of database structure including table relationships, row counts, and data quality metrics.
use_cases.target_user: Database administrator working with undocumented systems

prompt_templates.title: Basic Table Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: What tables exist in this database and how many rows does each have?

prompt_templates.title: Schema Deep Dive
prompt_templates.prompt: Explore the schema and give me a complete profile of the [table_name] table including columns, data types, row count, and data quality metrics.
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed understanding of a specific table

prompt_templates.title: Relationship Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the database schema and identify all foreign key relationships between tables. Show how tables connect to each other.

prompt_templates.title: Quality Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Profile all tables in the schema and identify any data quality issues - columns with high null rates, empty tables, or inconsistent data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Database health check before migration or analysis

output_examples.input: What tables exist in this database?
output_examples.output:
Found 5 tables in the public schema:

1. users (12,450 rows) - Core user accounts
2. orders (48,923 rows) - Customer orders
3. products (3,200 rows) - Product catalog
4. order_items (145,200 rows) - Line items per order
5. categories (150 rows) - Product categories

Key relationships detected:
- orders.user_id → users.id
- order_items.order_id → orders.id
- order_items.product_id → products.id
- products.category_id → categories.id

output_examples.input: Profile the users table for data quality
output_examples.output:
Data Quality Report: users table

Total rows: 12,450

Column completeness:
✓ id: 100% (12,450/12,450)
✓ email: 99.8% (12,425/12,450) - 25 nulls
✓ created_at: 100% (12,450/12,450)
⚠ phone: 45.2% (5,627/12,450) - Low completeness
⚠ address: 62.8% (7,818/12,450) - Medium completeness

Quality flags:
- Email column has 25 null values that may need attention
- Phone number data is sparse - optional field?
- Suggested query: Investigate users without email addresses

best_practices: Start with broad schema discovery using read_schema before diving into specific tables
Always check row counts before running expensive aggregation queries on large tables
Use data quality scoring to prioritize which tables and columns need cleaning
Document discovered relationships for future reference - they are not always explicitly defined as foreign keys
When exploring large databases, focus on one schema or table at a time rather than trying to profile everything at once
anti_patterns: Running COUNT(*) on very large tables without first understanding table size or using sampling
Assuming column names ending in '_id' are foreign keys without verifying the target table exists
Profiling every table in a large database simultaneously - can overwhelm the system and produce unmanageable output
Treating high null rates as data quality issues without understanding business context - some fields are legitimately optional

faq.question: Does this skill modify my database data?
faq.answer: No, this skill only performs read operations for exploration and profiling. It uses SELECT queries to gather information about schema structure, row counts, and data quality metrics.

faq.question: Can I explore multiple schemas at once?
faq.answer: Yes, you can run read_schema for each schema name you want to explore. The skill will analyze each schema separately and can help you understand relationships across schemas.

faq.question: How long does profiling take?
faq.answer: It depends on database size and table complexity. Small tables (thousands of rows) profile instantly. Large tables (millions of rows) may take longer due to COUNT(*) queries for statistics.

faq.question: What database systems are supported?
faq.answer: This skill works with any database connected to DBX Studio, including PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and others. The SQL queries generated use standard SQL syntax compatible with most systems.

faq.question: Can it detect application-level relationships?
faq.answer: The skill can identify foreign key constraints and naming patterns like columns ending in '_id'. However, application-level relationships not enforced at the database level may not be automatically detected.

faq.question: What should I do after data exploration?
faq.answer: After profiling, you can use the data-visualization skill to create charts of key insights, or the sql-queries skill to build more complex analyses based on discovered relationships.
